Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (18): File Handling
This time I want to talk about doing some file handling in Python — things like creating a file, writing data into a specific file, and so on.
Python comes with built-in functions for file handling. But what can file handling actually do for you? Let me give you a scenario.

Say you wrote a scraper today, and it went and crawled some site (maybe iThome Help). Once it's done crawling, you need to store the data somewhere — and that's where creating files and writing to files comes in.
So file handling is one of those things you kind of can't get away with not knowing.
You, hearing you have to learn yet another new thing
Now you might ask: can JavaScript do something like file handling too? Normally, no — unless... you're using Node.js.

So we tried opening a file and reading its content. Next up is writing to a file — because only opening and reading without ever writing is kind of weird, right?
Either way, like I mentioned, anything involving file operations always has an

So how do we open the file and write text into it?
Let's say we want to write two lines: "I'm Ray" and "My blog is
function comes in, but before using

And conversely, if you try to read while in a write mode, you'll get a different error:
mode is that once you open the file and write, it writes starting from
the end of the file's content
So when I run this code:

f = open('filename.txt', 'a')
f.write('I am Ray')
f.write('My blog is https://israynotarray.com/')
f.close()
If what you wanted was a line break, just add a newline character

f = open('filename.txt', 'a')
f.write('I am Ray\n')
f.write('My blog is https://israynotarray.com/')
f.close()
mode clears the file contents first and then starts writing.

Hello Python
I am Ray
My blog is https://israynotarray.com/
After I run this code, it changes:

f = open('filename.txt', 'w')
f.write('I am Ray\n')
f.write('My blog is https://israynotarray.com/')
f.close()
mode wipes the content before writing. If you don't have these two modes straight in your head, you might run into "why does my file keep getting wiped?" situations.
Last thing I want to add is something called plus mode. Above we talked about a bunch of file handling stuff, including how to write and how things work. As it turns out, the
function has even more modes you can use, and these are called "plus modes" (the ones with a
Using them is super simple — you just add a
sign to change the original mode.
means read-only, so you can't write. But once you use the plus mode
, it becomes a read-and-write state, meaning you can write to and read from the file at the same time.
What does that look like? Here's a quick example:

f = open('filename.txt', 'r+')
f.write('I am Ray\n')
f.write('My blog is https://israynotarray.com/')
f.close()
This code runs just fine and won't throw
plus mode too, but in my testing
didn't really differ much — one still clears the contents before writing, and the other still writes at the end. The one that actually makes a difference is
mode, so I won't add more on the rest.
When to use plus modes? There's no hard rule — it really comes down to what you need.

This time we're going to talk about doing some file handling in Python — things like creating a file and writing data into a specific file. We'll cover open(), the different modes (r, w, a), reading and writing, and at the end the "plus" modes like r+ that let you read and write at the same time.
